name: Pantagruel
alternative_names: Son of the Great Giant Gargantua
primary_sources: The Five Books of the Lives and Deeds of Gargantua and Pantagruel by François Rabelais

distinctive_features: ability to shelter an army with his tongue
mouth large enough for a person to travel into
visual_attributes.structural_arrangement: proportional to a human but on a much larger scale

domain_specific_visual.literary_exaggeration: features exaggerated for comedic and satirical effect

secondary_functions: vehicle for social and political commentary

domain_specific_functional.satirical_role: embodies the absurdity of human nature and society
domain_specific_functional.narrative_function: drives the plot through adventures and interactions

relationships.description: Pantagruel is the son of Gargantua, another giant.
Pantagruel travels with Panurge on various adventures.

contextual_attributes.cultural_significance: represents the human condition through satire and humor
primary_sources: The Five Books of the Lives and Deeds of Gargantua and Pantagruel

contrastive_constraints: NOT a modern giant like those in fantasy films
NOT a creature from non-European folklore
